title: Preparing water butts for winter
subtitle: Draining, cleaning, and protecting butts from frost cracking
excerpt: A water butt left full in winter risks frost damage. Water expands by about 9 percent when it freezes. A rigid plastic butt with nowhere for the ice to expand can crack along seam lines or at the tap fitting. Draining the butt down to one-third capacity and cleaning it before the first frost takes 20 minutes and prevents damage that would cost more to fix than the butt cost.
sourceNotes: WRAP water butt maintenance guidance. Water UK guidance on garden water storage. Manufacturer instructions for Graf Garantia and Ward Hippo water butts. Energy Saving Trust water efficiency guidance.

content.text: Most cracked butts are cracked by frost. The butt was left full during a cold snap. The water at the bottom froze first; the ice had nowhere to go; the seam split. The tap fitting is the other weak point: the thread often shears if the tap is left in and ice forms around it.

In the UK, complete winterisation before the end of November. In Scotland and upland England, aim for late October. The first sharp frost often comes without much warning; a butt that is still full when it arrives is at risk.

Drain the butt down to roughly one-third capacity. Use the remaining water on the garden. You do not need to fully empty it: a partially full butt has room for expansion and is less likely to freeze solid.
Close the tap at the base of the butt. Remove any overflow pipe if it can freeze and block.

Clean the inside of the butt if it has algae, silt, or slime build-up. Use a long-handled brush and a dilute bleach solution (1 part bleach to 100 parts water). Rinse twice with fresh water before refilling in spring.
Disconnect the diverter from the downpipe if the diverter is the type that reroutes overflow back to the drainpipe. Reconnect in spring.
Cover the top of the butt with a piece of board or a close-fitting lid to prevent rainwater topping it up during frost periods.

Polyethylene (flexible) butts are more frost-tolerant than rigid PVC. The butt walls flex slightly when the water expands. Even so, a full flexible butt left at minus 10°C will crack. Half-empty is safer.

In March, check the tap thread for cracks or stripped thread. Replace the tap if damaged (tap kits cost 3 to 5 pounds from garden centres). Reconnect the diverter and rinse the butt before collecting the first spring rainwater. If the butt spent winter with a residue of stagnant water, clean and rinse before use.
